threats.title: sqrt(0) Executes STOP Opcode Halting Entire Calling Transaction
threats.hypothesis_description: The sqrt() function handles zero input with the EVM `stop()` opcode instead of returning packedFloat(0). The STOP opcode halts the entire execution context with a success status but empty return data. Any calling contract invoking sqrt(packedFloat.wrap(0)) will have all subsequent operations (state changes, token transfers, event emissions) silently skipped, yet the transaction reports success. This creates catastrophic state inconsistency in any integrating protocol.
threats.investigation_rationale: Zero is a perfectly valid mathematical input for square root. Any protocol computing sqrt of a value that could reach zero (e.g., collateral value, reserve ratio, price feed) will have its execution silently terminated mid-transaction, leaving state changes incomplete but appearing successful.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Assembly `if iszero(a) { stop() }` uses STOP opcode instead of returning zero

preconditions: Caller passes packedFloat.wrap(0) to sqrt()
No special handling in caller for empty return data

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: stop() opcode instead of return for zero input
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: stop() halts execution with success but no return data; calling contract continues past sqrt() call with uninitialized return value OR never reaches subsequent code
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should assign r := 0 and fall through to normal return, not use stop()

analysis_questions: What happens in Solidity when a library internal call executes stop()? Does the calling function proceed with a zero return value or is execution halted entirely?
Does the compiler inline the library function such that stop() terminates the entire transaction?
Could a protocol wrap this in a try/catch to detect the failure?
validation_requirements: Test sqrt(packedFloat.wrap(0)) and verify whether the calling function continues execution
Verify return value behavior when stop() is reached in an internal library call
Test with a contract that performs state changes after sqrt() to confirm they are skipped

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A DeFi protocol computes sqrt(collateralRatio) as part of a liquidation check. When collateral drops to zero, sqrt(0) executes stop(), silently terminating the liquidation transaction with success but never executing the actual liquidation logic. Funds become permanently locked.
prerequisites: Any integrating protocol that calls sqrt with potentially zero inputs

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete loss of funds in protocols where sqrt(0) silently terminates critical state-changing operations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of any protocol integrating Float128.sqrt
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires protocol upgrade to work around the bug

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for sqrt(0) behavior and execution continuation

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: N/A - issue is logic not gas
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Transaction appears successful on-chain, making detection extremely difficult

threats.title: le() and ge() Return False for Mathematically Equal Values with Different M/L Encodings
threats.hypothesis_description: The le() and ge() comparison functions use strict lt/gt instead of le/ge when comparing mantissas of equal exponents. After normalizing both operands to L-size, when two mathematically equal values have different original encodings (M=38-digit vs L=72-digit), the bitwise early-return check fails, and the strict comparison in the body also fails, causing le() and ge() to return false for equal values. Combined with eq() also returning false, this violates trichotomy: none of lt, eq, gt, le, ge return true.
threats.investigation_rationale: Arithmetic operations naturally produce M or L encoded results depending on exponent magnitude. Two different computation paths can produce the same mathematical value in different encodings. Any protocol threshold check using le()/ge() will fail for the boundary case.

proof_of_signal.pattern: le() uses `retVal := lt(aMan, bMan)` for same-exponent positive case instead of `retVal := iszero(gt(aMan, bMan))`

preconditions: Two packedFloats represent the same mathematical value
One is M-encoded (38 digits) and the other is L-encoded (72 digits)
After L-normalization, mantissas and exponents are identical

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Using strict lt instead of le (and strict gt instead of ge) in mantissa comparison when exponents match
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: When aMan == bMan after normalization, lt(aMan, bMan) returns 0 (false), so le() returns false for equal values
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use `iszero(gt(aMan, bMan))` for le and `iszero(lt(aMan, bMan))` for ge, or `iszero(gt(bMan, aMan))` and `iszero(gt(aMan, bMan))` respectively

analysis_questions: Can arithmetic operations (add, sub, mul, div, sqrt) produce the same mathematical value in both M and L encodings?
What specific input combinations trigger cross-M/L encoding of the same value?
Does the MAXIMUM_EXPONENT auto-scaling create scenarios where the same value gets different encodings?
validation_requirements: Construct two packedFloats representing the same value in M and L encodings using toPackedFloat
Verify le(mEncoded, lEncoded) returns false when it should return true
Verify ge(mEncoded, lEncoded) also returns false
Verify the trichotomy violation: none of lt, eq, gt return true

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A lending protocol checks `le(borrowValue, maxBorrow)` to enforce borrowing limits. If borrowValue is computed via multiplication (producing L encoding) and maxBorrow was set via direct encoding (M encoding), and they are mathematically equal, the check fails - the user is denied borrowing at exactly the limit. Conversely, ge() checks for undercollateralization at the boundary fail, allowing positions that should be liquidated to persist.
prerequisites: Protocol uses le() or ge() for threshold/boundary checks
Values from different computation paths can represent the same number

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Boundary condition failures in all protocol invariant checks using these comparisons
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users at exact threshold boundaries

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing cross-M/L encoding comparison tests for equal values

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Deterministic bug, easily reproducible with specific input pairs

threats.title: eq() Uses Bitwise Comparison Failing for Same Value with Different M/L Encoding
threats.hypothesis_description: The eq() function performs a raw uint256 bitwise comparison (`packedFloat.unwrap(a) == packedFloat.unwrap(b)`) instead of mathematical value comparison. Two packedFloats representing the exact same mathematical value but encoded with different mantissa sizes (M=38 digits, L=72 digits) will have completely different bit patterns and eq() returns false. This violates reflexivity-under-value-equivalence for any protocol comparing computed results.
threats.investigation_rationale: The same number can be encoded as M (mantissa 10^37, exponent e) or L (mantissa 10^71, exponent e-34). These have different bit patterns due to different mantissa, exponent, and L-flag values. Any equality check between values from different computation paths will fail.

proof_of_signal.pattern: eq() implementation is `retVal = packedFloat.unwrap(a) == packedFloat.unwrap(b)` - pure bitwise comparison

preconditions: Two packedFloats represent the same mathematical value
They have different M/L encodings (one 38-digit, one 72-digit mantissa)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Bitwise uint256 comparison instead of value-based comparison
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: 1.0 encoded as M-size (mantissa=10^37, rawExp=8155) vs L-size (mantissa=10^71, rawExp=8121) produces completely different uint256 values
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should normalize both to same size before comparing, or compare decoded (mantissa, exponent) pairs

analysis_questions: How frequently do arithmetic operations produce cross-M/L encodings for the same value?
Does the auto-scaling at MAXIMUM_EXPONENT boundary create natural M/L encoding divergence?
Would fixing eq() also require fixing le() and ge()?
validation_requirements: Create toPackedFloat(1, 0) and toPackedFloat(int(10^71), -71) and verify eq() returns false
Verify both decode to the same mathematical value
Test with values produced by actual arithmetic operations

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A protocol checks `eq(currentRate, targetRate)` to determine if an interest rate has been reached. If currentRate is computed through division (producing L encoding) and targetRate was stored from a different calculation (M encoding), the equality check never triggers even when rates are identical, preventing rate adjustments or triggering incorrect fallback behavior.
prerequisites: Protocol uses eq() for value matching
Compared values originate from different computation paths

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Failed equality checks bypass protocol invariants and conditional logic
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users affected by equality-gated protocol logic

threats.title: add/sub exp(BASE, adj) Overflow Produces Corrupted Results for Large Exponent Differences
threats.hypothesis_description: In add() and sub(), when the exponent difference (adj) between operands exceeds ~77, the EVM `exp(10, adj)` opcode overflows uint256 and returns a wrapped modular value. For adj in [78, ~255], `sdiv(bMan, wrappedValue)` produces a non-zero garbage value instead of the mathematically correct 0, corrupting the addition/subtraction result. The smaller operand contributes a large wrong value to the sum instead of being negligible.
threats.investigation_rationale: Numbers with exponent differences of 78+ are well within the library's stated operating range [-3000, 3000]. A protocol adding total supply (10^18) to precision dust (10^-100) triggers this. The EVM exp opcode performs modular arithmetic: 10^78 mod 2^256 is non-zero and unpredictable, making sdiv return garbage.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `bMan := sdiv(bMan, exp(BASE, adj))` where adj can exceed 77, causing exp(10, adj) to overflow uint256

preconditions: Two operands with exponent difference > 77 (for M-size numbers)
Both operands are non-zero
adj falls in the range [78, ~255] where exp returns non-zero wrapped values

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: Float128.sol lines 80-88 (add, M-size branch when aExp > bExp)
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: EVM exp opcode modular overflow for exponents > 77
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: exp(10, 78) = 10^78 mod 2^256 is non-zero (~10^78 - 2^256*k), sdiv(mantissa, this_value) returns unpredictable non-zero result
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that adj <= 77 before calling exp; should clamp adj or set smaller mantissa to 0 when adj exceeds digit count

analysis_questions: For which adj values in [78, 255] does exp(10, adj) return non-zero values?
What is the magnitude of the corruption for typical mantissa values?
Does the same bug affect L-size number branches?
validation_requirements: Compute add(toPackedFloat(1, 50), toPackedFloat(1, -50)) and verify correctness
Test with exponent differences of 78, 100, 150, 200, 255, 256
Verify the result deviates significantly from the expected value

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A protocol computes totalValue = add(largeAssetValue, smallDustAmount) where the exponent difference exceeds 77. The exp overflow causes the dust amount to contribute a large garbage value, inflating totalValue. An attacker deposits a tiny amount of a low-decimal token and withdraws based on the corrupted total, extracting excess funds.
prerequisites: Numbers with >77 orders of magnitude difference in the same calculation

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Completely wrong arithmetic results for operands with large exponent differences
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of protocols performing arithmetic on values spanning many orders of magnitude

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for addition with large exponent differences (>77)

threats.title: ln() Silently Accepts Negative Inputs Instead of Reverting
threats.hypothesis_description: The ln() function extracts the mantissa using `and(input, MANTISSA_MASK)` which strips the sign bit (bit 240) without checking it. For negative inputs, ln() silently computes ln(|input|) and returns a valid positive result. Natural logarithm is mathematically undefined for negative real numbers. This masks upstream bugs where a negative value reaches ln(), propagating incorrect but plausible-looking results through financial calculations.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unlike sqrt() which properly validates the sign bit and reverts for negative inputs, ln() completely ignores the sign. If an upstream subtraction produces a slightly negative intermediate value (e.g., negative interest rate or negative price), ln() will return a normal-looking result instead of reverting, allowing the computation to continue with wrong values.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ln() extracts mantissa with `and(input, MANTISSA_MASK)` ignoring sign bit at position 240; no sign validation

preconditions: A negative packedFloat is passed to ln()
The calling protocol does not independently validate the input sign

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Sign bit ignored during mantissa extraction; no revert for negative input
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: For input = -5.0 (sign bit set, mantissa 5*10^37), ln() computes ln(5) and returns ~1.609, silently ignoring the negative sign
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing check similar to sqrt: `if and(input, MANTISSA_SIGN_MASK) { revert(...) }`

analysis_questions: Does ln() produce an incorrect sign in the result for negative inputs, or always positive?
What happens when ln(negative) result is used in subsequent calculations?
Is there any downstream protocol that could pass a negative value to ln()?
validation_requirements: Call Ln.ln(Float128.toPackedFloat(-5, 0)) and verify it returns ln(5) without reverting
Compare behavior with sqrt which correctly reverts on negative input
Test with various negative values including -1, -0.5, -100

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A protocol computes ln(reserveRatio) where reserveRatio can go negative during extreme market conditions. Instead of reverting and halting the bad-state calculation, ln() returns a valid-looking positive result. The protocol proceeds with a wrong interest rate or price, enabling value extraction by informed actors.
prerequisites: Protocol doesn't independently validate input domain

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Incorrect but plausible results propagate through financial formulas, masking upstream errors
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users affected by calculations depending on ln() output
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires library fix adding sign validation

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing negative input test for ln()

threats.title: Exponent Underflow in add/sub for Small Raw Exponents Produces Massive Wrong Exponent
threats.hypothesis_description: In add() and sub(), the assembly instruction `r := sub(aExp, shl(EXPONENT_BIT, MAX_DIGITS_M))` performs unsigned subtraction. When the raw exponent value packed in aExp is less than MAX_DIGITS_M (38), this underflows, wrapping to a massive uint256 value. The resulting exponent field is completely wrong, and all subsequent normalization operates on this corrupted value, producing a result with an astronomically incorrect order of magnitude.
threats.investigation_rationale: Numbers with effective exponents below approximately -8154 (raw exponent < 38) for M-size calculations will trigger this underflow. While extreme, these values are within the 14-bit exponent range and could occur in deeply nested calculations or scientific computing contexts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `r := sub(aExp, shl(EXPONENT_BIT, MAX_DIGITS_M))` underflows when raw packed exponent < 38; unsigned wrap produces huge value

preconditions: At least one operand has a very small raw exponent (effective exponent < -8154)
Both operands are M-size and non-zero
The operand with the larger exponent has raw exponent < 38

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unsigned subtraction `sub(aExp, shl(242, 38))` wraps for small aExp
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If aExp represents raw exponent 20 (effective -8172), sub produces 2^256 - (38-20)*2^242, completely corrupting the result exponent field
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No bounds check on raw exponent before subtraction; should check if raw exponent >= MAX_DIGITS_M

analysis_questions: What is the minimum raw exponent that avoids underflow for M-size?
Can toPackedFloat() create values with raw exponents below the underflow threshold?
Does the L-size branch have the same issue with DIGIT_DIFF_76_L?
validation_requirements: Create packedFloats with very small exponents (raw < 38)
Add two such values and verify the result is corrupted
Test with exponents at the boundary of the valid range

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Adding two extremely small numbers (exponents < -8154) produces a result with a wrapped exponent, appearing as an astronomically large number. If used in a balance or pricing calculation, this could create phantom value.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Completely wrong results for extreme-range arithmetic
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users of protocols operating at extreme value ranges

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing edge-case tests for minimum exponent values

threats.title: mul() Exponent Overflow Wraps 14-bit Exponent Field for Large-Exponent Products
threats.hypothesis_description: In mul(), the result exponent is computed as `rExp = aExp_raw + bExp_raw - ZERO_OFFSET + normalization_offset`. When multiplying two numbers with large positive exponents, the raw exponent sum can exceed the 14-bit field maximum (16383). When packed via `shl(EXPONENT_BIT, rExp)`, only the lower 14 bits are retained, causing the exponent to wrap around. A result that should have exponent +7808 could appear as -192, making the value off by ~10^8000.
threats.investigation_rationale: With exponent range [-8192, +8191], multiplying two numbers with exponents near +4000 produces a result exponent near +8000. The raw exponent = (8192+4000) + (8192+4000) - 8192 + norm ≈ 16192 + norm. If norm pushes this above 16383, the field wraps. The authors state exponents > 3000 are out of bounds, but the library doesn't enforce this.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `rExp := sub(add(shr(EXPONENT_BIT, aExp), shr(EXPONENT_BIT, bExp)), ZERO_OFFSET)` can exceed 14-bit field capacity

preconditions: Both operands have large positive effective exponents (each > ~4000)
Result exponent exceeds the 14-bit field maximum (16383)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No bounds checking on computed exponent before packing into 14-bit field
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: shl(242, rExp) where rExp > 16383 silently truncates to lower 14 bits via modular arithmetic
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No exponent overflow/underflow check; should revert if result exponent exceeds representable range

analysis_questions: What is the exact threshold for exponent overflow in mul()?
Does the same issue affect div() with extreme exponent differences?
Can the normalization offset push a borderline exponent over the threshold?
validation_requirements: Multiply two numbers with exponents near +4000 and verify result exponent is correct
Test with exponents at +4091 and +4092 (sum = 16383/16384 raw)
Verify the result is incorrect when exponent wraps

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Multiplying two large-exponent values produces a result whose exponent wraps from a huge positive to a negative, making the result appear tiny instead of enormous. A protocol computing compound interest over extreme periods could get a near-zero result instead of the correct large value.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Results off by many orders of magnitude for extreme-exponent multiplication
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users of protocols with extreme value ranges
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires library fix adding exponent bounds checking

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for extreme exponent multiplication

threats.title: sqrt() Heron's Method Branch Exponent Underflow for Small Exponents
threats.hypothesis_description: In the sqrt() Heron's method branch (else path), the assembly code performs `aExp := sub(aExp, MAX_DIGITS_M)` or `sub(aExp, MAX_DIGITS_M_PLUS_1)`. When the raw exponent is small (below MAX_DIGITS_M=38 or MAX_DIGITS_M_PLUS_1=39), this unsigned subtraction wraps to a huge value. The subsequent `div(sub(aExp, ZERO_OFFSET), 2)` performs unsigned division on the wrapped value, producing a completely wrong result exponent.
threats.investigation_rationale: This branch handles numbers with effective exponents below approximately -8154 (when the condition for the 512-bit branch is not met). The unsigned arithmetic on the exponent has no bounds checking, and the wrap-around propagates through the halving and offset operations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `aExp := sub(aExp, MAX_DIGITS_M)` underflows for aExp < 38; then `aExp := add(div(sub(aExp, ZERO_OFFSET), 2), ZERO_OFFSET)` operates on wrapped value

preconditions: Input has a very small raw exponent (< 38)
Input enters the Heron's method branch (else path in sqrt)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: aExp wraps to ~2^256 - small_value, then div(sub(huge_value, 8192), 2) produces ~2^255, completely wrong exponent
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No bounds check ensuring aExp >= MAX_DIGITS_M before subtraction

analysis_questions: Which inputs reach the Heron's method branch with raw exponents < 38?
Does the 512-bit branch condition correctly redirect most small-exponent inputs?
What is the correct behavior for sqrt of numbers near the minimum representable value?
validation_requirements: Construct inputs with very small exponents that enter the Heron's branch
Verify the result exponent is wrapped/incorrect
Compare with expected mathematical result

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Computing sqrt of an extremely small number (e.g., near minimum representable) in the Heron's method branch produces a result with a completely wrong exponent, appearing as a very large number instead of a small one.
prerequisites: Input with effective exponent < -8154 reaching Heron's branch

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Incorrect sqrt results for extreme-range inputs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocols using sqrt with potentially very small values

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing edge-case tests for sqrt with minimum exponent values

threats.title: div() Unchecked Exponent Arithmetic Wraps for Extreme Value Divisions
threats.hypothesis_description: In div(), for L-operations, the result exponent uses `unchecked { rExp = (aExp + ZERO_OFFSET) - bExp; }`. When dividing a very small number by a very large one (or vice versa), this calculation can underflow or overflow. The `unchecked` block explicitly suppresses Solidity 0.8's arithmetic checks. The non-L path uses assembly `rExp := sub(add(aExp, ZERO_OFFSET), bExp)` which also has no overflow protection.
threats.investigation_rationale: Dividing a number with exponent -3000 by one with exponent +3000 produces result exponent ≈ -6000. The raw calculation: aExp ≈ 5192, bExp ≈ 11192, rExp = (5192 + 8192) - 11192 = 2192. This is fine. But dividing exponent -8000 by +8000: aExp ≈ 192, bExp ≈ 16192, rExp = (192 + 8192) - 16192 = -7808 which wraps in uint256.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `unchecked { rExp = (aExp + ZERO_OFFSET) - bExp; }` can underflow for extreme exponent divisions

preconditions: Numerator has very small exponent and denominator has very large exponent (or vice versa)
Result exponent exceeds representable range

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Explicit `unchecked` block suppresses overflow protection on exponent calculation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Result rExp can wrap to huge value when dividing small by large, producing result with wrong order of magnitude
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No bounds checking on result exponent; unchecked block intentionally disables Solidity protections

analysis_questions: At what exponent combinations does rExp underflow?
Does the packed 14-bit field mask the issue or make it worse?
What is the minimum exponent difference that triggers the underflow?
validation_requirements: Divide toPackedFloat(1, -5000) by toPackedFloat(1, 5000) and check result
Test at boundary values where rExp = 0 and rExp = -1 (underflow)
Verify result exponent is incorrect after packing

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Dividing an extremely small number by an extremely large one produces a result with a wrapped exponent, appearing as a very large number instead of an even smaller one.
prerequisites: Extreme exponent values in numerator and denominator

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Incorrect division results at extreme value ranges
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocols performing division with extreme-range values

threats.title: Normalization Invariant Violation in add/sub Overflow-by-One Case
threats.hypothesis_description: In the addition normalization path of add() and sub(), the code checks if the result exceeds MAX_L_DIGIT_NUMBER or falls between MIN_L_DIGIT_NUMBER and MAX_M_DIGIT_NUMBER. However, there may be edge cases where the addition of two normalized mantissas produces a result with 39 or 73 digits that is not properly caught by the normalization logic, resulting in a denormalized packedFloat. Subsequent operations on this denormalized value will silently produce incorrect results because all arithmetic assumes exactly 38 or 72 digits.
threats.investigation_rationale: The addition normalization has complex branching between M and L decisions. The interaction between the `_isM` flag, the `isGreaterThan76Digits` check, and the final digit-count adjustment could miss certain combinations. A mantissa of exactly MAX_M_DIGIT_NUMBER + 1 (10^38) that should be L but falls into the M normalization path would lose the most significant digit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Complex branching in addition normalization: _isM decision, digit count checks, and final truncation may have edge cases producing denormalized results

preconditions: Two operands whose mantissa sum is at a boundary (e.g., exactly 10^38 or 10^72)
The normalization logic selects M-path but the result requires L-path or vice versa

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Boundary condition in maxExp calculation interacting with isGreaterThan76Digits flag
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The `_isM := or(eq(rExp, maxExp), lt(rExp, maxExp))` check may misclassify when rExp is exactly at the boundary, causing wrong normalization path
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No post-normalization assertion that the mantissa is within [MIN_M, MAX_M] or [MIN_L, MAX_L]

analysis_questions: Are there input pairs where the addition produces exactly 10^38, 10^72, or 10^76 intermediate mantissa?
Does the _isM boundary correctly handle all intermediate mantissa sizes?
Can the subtraction normalization path produce denormalized results for catastrophic cancellation cases?
validation_requirements: Fuzz test add/sub with random inputs and verify all outputs are properly normalized
Test boundary inputs where the mantissa sum is exactly at normalization thresholds
Verify invariant: output mantissa ∈ [MIN_M, MAX_M] ∪ [MIN_L, MAX_L] ∪ {0}

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a denormalized result is produced, subsequent multiplication using this value will have the wrong number of digits in the mantissa, causing intermediate results to overflow uint256 or lose precision. In a multi-step financial calculation, this compounds into a significantly wrong final value.
prerequisites: Specific input values that trigger normalization boundary edge cases

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Cascading precision errors in multi-step calculations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of protocols with multi-step floating-point calculations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires library fix adding post-normalization assertions

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing normalization boundary tests and invariant assertions

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Requires systematic boundary testing or fuzzing

threats.title: ln() Recursive Call May Not Terminate for Specific Crafted Inputs
threats.hypothesis_description: The ln_helper() function recursively calls ln() when the input's positive exponent indicates a value > 1.0 (first branch). The recursion assumes that computing the reciprocal (one_over_arguments_76) produces a value <= 1.0 that takes the non-recursive second branch. If precision errors in the reciprocal computation or edge cases in the toPackedFloat encoding cause the recursive argument to still appear > 1.0, infinite recursion ensues until gas is exhausted.
threats.investigation_rationale: The reciprocal is computed via integer division and encoded via toPackedFloat. If the input is very close to 1.0 but > 1.0, the reciprocal is close to but < 1.0, and its exponent should make it take the else branch. But if precision in the reciprocal causes it to appear >= 1.0 in the encoded form, the recursion doesn't terminate.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Recursive `ln()` call in ln_helper first branch; termination depends on reciprocal value always entering second branch

preconditions: Input value > 1.0 that triggers the first branch
The reciprocal computation produces a value that re-triggers the first branch
This would require a precision error in the reciprocal or edge case in encoding

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Recursive call to ln() with computed reciprocal; no recursion depth limit
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No explicit guard ensuring recursion depth = 1; relies on mathematical property that may not hold for all encoded values
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No recursion depth counter or explicit check that recursive argument is < 1.0

analysis_questions: For which inputs does the reciprocal computation in ln_helper first branch produce a value with positiveExp < MAX_DIGITS?
Can the reciprocal of a value slightly > 1.0 be encoded with a positive effective exponent?
Is there a mathematical proof that the recursion always terminates in exactly 1 step?
validation_requirements: Test ln() with values very close to 1.0 (e.g., 1 + 10^-37)
Test ln() with very large values (e.g., 10^3000)
Monitor recursion depth for a wide range of inputs

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If an input exists that causes infinite recursion, any protocol calling ln() on that input would have the transaction revert with out-of-gas. If ln() is used in a critical path (e.g., liquidation calculation), this creates a denial-of-service preventing the protocol operation.
prerequisites: Specific input value that causes reciprocal to re-trigger first branch

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: DoS on protocol operations depending on ln()
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of affected protocol operations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Protocol must avoid the triggering input value

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing recursion depth and termination tests

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Gas consumption of recursive ln() calls
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable via gas exhaustion in transaction

threats.title: sub(0, b) Returns Non-Canonical Representation When b Has Non-Canonical Form
threats.hypothesis_description: In sub(), when a=0 and b is non-zero, the code XORs the sign bit: `b := xor(MANTISSA_SIGN_MASK, b)`. If b is a non-canonical packedFloat (e.g., zero mantissa with non-zero exponent/flags), the XOR produces another non-canonical value that propagates through the system. Additionally, if b itself was obtained from a buggy operation that produced a non-canonical form, sub(0, b) preserves and propagates the corruption with only a sign flip.
threats.investigation_rationale: The documentation states zero must be represented as all-zero bits. But if any code path (including external callers using packedFloat.wrap) creates a non-canonical zero, sub(0, nonCanonicalZero) returns the non-canonical zero with flipped sign. This non-canonical value then fails comparison checks against canonical zero.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `gt(b, 0)` checks raw uint256, not mantissa; XOR sign on any non-zero packed value including non-canonical zeros

preconditions: First operand is zero (canonical)
Second operand is a non-canonical packedFloat (e.g., zero mantissa but non-zero exponent bits)
Non-canonical value was created by external code or propagated from a normalization bug

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: `gt(b, 0)` is true for any non-zero uint256, even if mantissa field is 0
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: A packedFloat with mantissa=0 but exponent bits set passes the gt check; XOR produces non-canonical negative-zero-with-exponent
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should check mantissa field specifically, or validate that b represents a valid non-zero number

analysis_questions: Can any internal Float128 operation produce a non-canonical zero (mantissa=0, other bits set)?
If so, does sub(0, nonCanonicalZero) propagate the corruption?
Do comparison functions handle non-canonical zeros correctly?
validation_requirements: Create a non-canonical zero via packedFloat.wrap and test sub(0, nonCanonicalZero)
Verify the result is non-canonical
Test if the result behaves correctly in subsequent comparisons and arithmetic

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a protocol constructs packedFloats directly via wrap (or if a normalization bug produces non-canonical zeros), sub(0, value) propagates the non-canonical form. Subsequent eq(result, 0) returns false, potentially causing balance checks to fail or invariant violations.
prerequisites: Non-canonical packedFloat exists in the system

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Propagation of corrupted values through arithmetic chain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users whose operations encounter non-canonical values
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires library fix adding mantissa validation
